title: Agreement (Offer and Acceptance)
text: Agreement (Offer and Acceptance): A contract is a legally enforceable agreement, and the existence of an agreement is determined objectively based on what a reasonable person would conclude from the parties' words and actions.
summary: A contract is a legally enforceable agreement, and the existence of an agreement is determined objectively based on what a reasonable person would conclude from the parties' words and actions.

title: Offer vs. Invitation to Treat
text: Offer vs. Invitation to Treat: An offer must be distinguished from an invitation to treat; for example, the display of goods with a price tag or an advertisement is generally merely an invitation to treat, not a binding offer.
summary: An offer must be distinguished from an invitation to treat; for example, the display of goods with a price tag or an advertisement is generally merely an invitation to treat, not a binding offer.

title: Communication of Acceptance
text: Communication of Acceptance: Acceptance must generally be communicated to the offeror, but under the "postal rule," acceptance by post may be effective upon mailing. For electronic communications, receipt may depend on whether an "information system" was designated under the Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553).
summary: Acceptance must generally be communicated to the offeror, but under the "postal rule," acceptance by post may be effective upon mailing. For electronic communications, receipt may depend on whether an "information system" was designated under the Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553).

title: Consideration
text: Consideration: A fundamental requirement for all contracts not made under seal is consideration, which must be "sufficient" (of some value in the eyes of the law) but need not be "adequate" (of equal value). Past consideration is generally not valid consideration. However, the performance of an existing contractual duty may constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor, following the principle in Williams v Roffey Bros.
summary: A fundamental requirement for all contracts not made under seal is consideration, which must be "sufficient" (of some value in the eyes of the law) but need not be "adequate" (of equal value). Past consideration is generally not valid consideration. However, the performance of an existing contractual duty may constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor, following the principl...

title: Intention to Create Legal Relations
text: Intention to Create Legal Relations: For an agreement to be enforceable, the parties must intend to create legal relations, which is presumed in commercial contexts but generally presumed absent in social or domestic arrangements.
summary: For an agreement to be enforceable, the parties must intend to create legal relations, which is presumed in commercial contexts but generally presumed absent in social or domestic arrangements.

title: Capacity
text: Capacity: Exceptions to full contractual capacity include minors, drunkards, and those with mental incapacity. Contracts for "necessaries" or beneficial contracts of employment are enforceable against minors, who must pay a reasonable price for them.
summary: Exceptions to full contractual capacity include minors, drunkards, and those with mental incapacity. Contracts for "necessaries" or beneficial contracts of employment are enforceable against minors, who must pay a reasonable price for them.

title: Formality
text: Formality: The general rule is that contracts can be made in any form, including orally. A major exception is contracts for the sale or disposition of land, which must be in writing or evidenced by a signed memorandum under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. Guarantees no longer need to be in writing under Hong Kong law.
summary: The general rule is that contracts can be made in any form, including orally. A major exception is contracts for the sale or disposition of land, which must be in writing or evidenced by a signed memorandum under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. Guarantees no longer need to be in writing under Hong Kong law.
citations: The general rule is that contracts can be made in any form, including orally. A major exception is contracts for the sale or disposition of land, which must be in writing or evidenced by a signed memorandum under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219)

title: Express Terms and the Parol Evidence Rule
text: Express Terms and the Parol Evidence Rule: Where parties have reduced their agreement to writing, the parol evidence rule generally prevents the admission of extrinsic or oral evidence to add to, vary, or contradict the written terms.
summary: Where parties have reduced their agreement to writing, the parol evidence rule generally prevents the admission of extrinsic or oral evidence to add to, vary, or contradict the written terms.

title: Implied Terms
text: Implied Terms: Terms may be implied by custom, in law, by statute, or in fact to give "business efficacy" to the contract or because they are so obvious they "go without saying" (the officious bystander test).
summary: Terms may be implied by custom, in law, by statute, or in fact to give "business efficacy" to the contract or because they are so obvious they "go without saying" (the officious bystander test).

title: Statutory Implied Terms
text: Statutory Implied Terms: The Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) implies conditions that the seller has the right to sell, and that goods will correspond with their description, be of merchantable quality, and be fit for purpose. The Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457) implies terms that services will be carried out with reasonable care and skill, within a reasonable time, and for a reasonable charge.
summary: The Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) implies conditions that the seller has the right to sell, and that goods will correspond with their description, be of merchantable quality, and be fit for purpose. The Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457) implies terms that services will be carried out with reasonable care and skill, within a reasonable time, and for a reasonable charge.
citations: The Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) implies conditions that the seller has the right to sell, and that goods will correspond with their description, be of merchantable quality, and be fit for purpose. The Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457)

title: Classification of Terms
text: Classification of Terms:
summary: Grouping node inside Contractual Terms.

title: Conditions
text: Conditions: Essential terms; any breach entitles the innocent party to terminate the contract and claim damages.
summary: Essential terms; any breach entitles the innocent party to terminate the contract and claim damages.

title: Warranties
text: Warranties: Lesser terms; breach entitles the innocent party only to claim damages, not to terminate.
summary: Lesser terms; breach entitles the innocent party only to claim damages, not to terminate.

title: Innominate (Intermediate) Terms
text: Innominate (Intermediate) Terms: The right to terminate depends on whether the consequences of the breach deprive the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract.
summary: The right to terminate depends on whether the consequences of the breach deprive the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract.

title: Exemption Clauses
text: Exemption Clauses: Clauses attempting to exclude or restrict liability are construed strictly against the party relying on them (the contra proferentem rule).
summary: Clauses attempting to exclude or restrict liability are construed strictly against the party relying on them (the contra proferentem rule).

title: Statutory Control of Exemption Clauses
text: Statutory Control of Exemption Clauses: Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71), a party cannot exclude liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. Excluding other negligence liability, or liability for breach of contract when dealing on standard terms or with a consumer, is only valid if the clause satisfies the statutory test of "reasonableness".
summary: Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71), a party cannot exclude liability for death or personal injury resulting from negligence. Excluding other negligence liability, or liability for breach of contract when dealing on standard terms or with a consumer, is only valid if the clause satisfies the statutory test of "reasonableness".
citations: Under the Control of Exemption Clauses Ordinance (Cap. 71)

title: Misrepresentation
text: Misrepresentation: A false statement of fact that induces a party to enter into a contract renders the contract voidable. Remedies include rescission and damages under the Misrepresentation Ordinance (Cap. 284), which allows damages for negligent misrepresentation unless the representor proves they had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true.
summary: A false statement of fact that induces a party to enter into a contract renders the contract voidable. Remedies include rescission and damages under the Misrepresentation Ordinance (Cap. 284), which allows damages for negligent misrepresentation unless the representor proves they had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true.
citations: A false statement of fact that induces a party to enter into a contract renders the contract voidable. Remedies include rescission and damages under the Misrepresentation Ordinance (Cap. 284)

title: Mistake
text: Mistake: A contract may be void if it is affected by an operative mistake, including common mistake (both parties make the same fundamental mistake), mutual mistake (parties are at cross-purposes), or unilateral mistake (one party is mistaken as to the terms or identity, and the other knows it).
summary: A contract may be void if it is affected by an operative mistake, including common mistake (both parties make the same fundamental mistake), mutual mistake (parties are at cross-purposes), or unilateral mistake (one party is mistaken as to the terms or identity, and the other knows it).

title: Duress
text: Duress: Illegitimate pressure, including economic duress, that coerces the will of a party and leaves them with no practical alternative makes the contract voidable.
summary: Illegitimate pressure, including economic duress, that coerces the will of a party and leaves them with no practical alternative makes the contract voidable.

title: Undue Influence
text: Undue Influence: An equitable doctrine rendering a contract voidable where consent is obtained through unacceptable influence; it can be actual or presumed from a relationship of trust and confidence (e.g., parent/child, solicitor/client) where the transaction calls for an explanation.
summary: An equitable doctrine rendering a contract voidable where consent is obtained through unacceptable influence; it can be actual or presumed from a relationship of trust and confidence (e.g., parent/child, solicitor/client) where the transaction calls for an explanation.

title: Unconscionability
text: Unconscionability: The Unconscionable Contracts Ordinance (Cap. 458) empowers courts to refuse to enforce, or to revise, unconscionable terms in contracts where one party deals as a consumer for goods or services. At common law, unconscionability applies sparingly where a party in a superior bargaining position abuses that position to produce an agreement with severe unfairness.
summary: The Unconscionable Contracts Ordinance (Cap. 458) empowers courts to refuse to enforce, or to revise, unconscionable terms in contracts where one party deals as a consumer for goods or services. At common law, unconscionability applies sparingly where a party in a superior bargaining position abuses that position to produce an agreement with severe unfairness.

title: Illegality
text: Illegality: Contracts that are illegal as formed (e.g., agreements to commit a crime, or unauthorized gambling under the Gambling Ordinance) or contrary to public policy are generally void and unenforceable.
summary: Contracts that are illegal as formed (e.g., agreements to commit a crime, or unauthorized gambling under the Gambling Ordinance) or contrary to public policy are generally void and unenforceable.

title: By Performance
text: By Performance: The general rule requires exact and precise performance of contractual obligations.
summary: The general rule requires exact and precise performance of contractual obligations.

title: By Breach
text: By Breach: A repudiatory breach, or a breach of a condition, gives the innocent party an "election": they may choose to affirm the contract or accept the repudiation to terminate the contract and claim damages.
summary: A repudiatory breach, or a breach of a condition, gives the innocent party an "election": they may choose to affirm the contract or accept the repudiation to terminate the contract and claim damages.

title: By Frustration
text: By Frustration: Occurs when a supervening, unforeseeable event, occurring without the fault of either party, makes performance impossible or radically transforms the nature of the contractual obligations. The Law Amendment and Reform (Consolidation) Ordinance (Cap. 23) grants courts discretion to adjust the financial rights and liabilities of the parties following frustration.
summary: Occurs when a supervening, unforeseeable event, occurring without the fault of either party, makes performance impossible or radically transforms the nature of the contractual obligations. The Law Amendment and Reform (Consolidation) Ordinance (Cap. 23) grants courts discretion to adjust the financial rights and liabilities of the parties following frustration.
citations: Occurs when a supervening, unforeseeable event, occurring without the fault of either party, makes performance impossible or radically transforms the nature of the contractual obligations. The Law Amendment and Reform (Consolidation) Ordinance (Cap. 23)

title: Unliquidated Damages
text: Unliquidated Damages: The primary common law remedy. The aim is compensatory: to put the innocent party in the position they would have been in had the contract been properly performed.
summary: The primary common law remedy. The aim is compensatory: to put the innocent party in the position they would have been in had the contract been properly performed.

title: Remoteness of Damage
text: Remoteness of Damage: Under the rule in Hadley v Baxendale, losses are only recoverable if they arise naturally in the ordinary course of things, or were reasonably foreseeable as a probable result of the breach at the time the contract was made.
summary: Under the rule in Hadley v Baxendale, losses are only recoverable if they arise naturally in the ordinary course of things, or were reasonably foreseeable as a probable result of the breach at the time the contract was made.
citations: Under the rule in Hadley v Baxendale

title: Liquidated Damages vs. Penalties
text: Liquidated Damages vs. Penalties: A liquidated damages clause specifying a sum payable upon breach is enforceable if it is a genuine pre-estimate of loss or protects a legitimate interest proportionally; if it is out of all proportion and penal in nature, it is an unenforceable penalty.
summary: A liquidated damages clause specifying a sum payable upon breach is enforceable if it is a genuine pre-estimate of loss or protects a legitimate interest proportionally; if it is out of all proportion and penal in nature, it is an unenforceable penalty.

title: Equitable Remedies
text: Equitable Remedies: Specific performance (an order to fulfill the contract) and injunctions (an order to refrain from breaching a negative obligation) are discretionary and generally only granted when monetary damages are inadequate.
summary: Specific performance (an order to fulfill the contract) and injunctions (an order to refrain from breaching a negative obligation) are discretionary and generally only granted when monetary damages are inadequate.

title: Restitution (Unjust Enrichment)
text: Restitution (Unjust Enrichment): Available exceptionally, such as when there has been a "total failure of basis" or consideration, allowing a party to recover money paid under a void or discharged contract.
summary: Available exceptionally, such as when there has been a "total failure of basis" or consideration, allowing a party to recover money paid under a void or discharged contract.

title: General Rule
text: General Rule: At common law, the doctrine of privity dictates that only a person who is a party to a contract can sue or be sued on it.
summary: At common law, the doctrine of privity dictates that only a person who is a party to a contract can sue or be sued on it.

title: Statutory Exceptions
text: Statutory Exceptions: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623) provides a major exception, allowing a third party to enforce a contractual term if the contract expressly provides that they may do so, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party, provided the third party is expressly identified.
summary: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623) provides a major exception, allowing a third party to enforce a contractual term if the contract expressly provides that they may do so, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party, provided the third party is expressly identified.
citations: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623)

title: Sale of Goods and Services
text: Sale of Goods and Services: Contracts are governed by the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) and the Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457), which imply strict conditions regarding title, description, merchantable quality, and reasonable care and skill. Key sub-topics include the passing of property/title, the nemo dat rule (a seller cannot pass better title than they have), and retention of title (Romalpa) clauses.
summary: Contracts are governed by the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) and the Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457), which imply strict conditions regarding title, description, merchantable quality, and reasonable care and skill. Key sub-topics include the passing of property/title, the nemo dat rule (a seller cannot pass better title than they have), and retention of title (Romalpa) clauses.
citations: Contracts are governed by the Sale of Goods Ordinance (Cap. 26) and the Supply of Services (Implied Terms) Ordinance (Cap. 457)

title: Sale of Land and Leases
text: Sale of Land and Leases: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be evidenced in writing under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. The framework should map Provisional Agreements for Sale and Purchase (PASP)—which are legally binding—and the vendor's implied duty to prove and give "good title".
summary: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be evidenced in writing under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219), subject to the equitable doctrine of part performance. The framework should map Provisional Agreements for Sale and Purchase (PASP)—which are legally binding—and the vendor's implied duty to prove and give "good title".
citations: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be evidenced in writing under section 3(1) of the Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219)

title: Employment Contracts
text: Employment Contracts: A critical distinction must be drawn between a contract of service (employer-employee, attracting vicarious liability and statutory protections) and a contract for services (independent contractor). These contracts feature an implied duty of mutual trust and confidence.
summary: A critical distinction must be drawn between a contract of service (employer-employee, attracting vicarious liability and statutory protections) and a contract for services (independent contractor). These contracts feature an implied duty of mutual trust and confidence.

title: Construction Contracts
text: Construction Contracts: These heavily rely on standard forms (e.g., HKIA, FIDIC, and Government forms). Key nodes include variations, certification by architects, performance bonds, and "retention" monies held until practical completion.
summary: These heavily rely on standard forms (e.g., HKIA, FIDIC, and Government forms). Key nodes include variations, certification by architects, performance bonds, and "retention" monies held until practical completion.

title: Credit, Security, and Guarantees
text: Credit, Security, and Guarantees: Differentiate between mortgages, charges (fixed and floating), pledges, and contractual liens. Link the Money Lenders Ordinance (Cap. 163), which polices extortionate interest rates and requires strict formalities for loan agreements. Also, map Suretyship (Guarantees vs. Indemnities) and "on demand" bonds.
summary: Differentiate between mortgages, charges (fixed and floating), pledges, and contractual liens. Link the Money Lenders Ordinance (Cap. 163), which polices extortionate interest rates and requires strict formalities for loan agreements. Also, map Suretyship (Guarantees vs. Indemnities) and "on demand" bonds.
citations: Differentiate between mortgages, charges (fixed and floating), pledges, and contractual liens. Link the Money Lenders Ordinance (Cap. 163)

title: Insurance Contracts
text: Insurance Contracts: Governed by the stringent duty of utmost good faith (uberrimae fidei), requiring full disclosure of material facts. The insured must have an "insurable interest" in the subject matter, distinguishing it from wagering contracts.
summary: Governed by the stringent duty of utmost good faith (uberrimae fidei), requiring full disclosure of material facts. The insured must have an "insurable interest" in the subject matter, distinguishing it from wagering contracts.

section_title: Cyber Law and Emerging Technologies (Modern Modalities)
title: Electronic Contracting
text: Electronic Contracting: Map how offer and acceptance apply to clickwrap and browsewrap agreements. The Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553) validates electronic records and electronic/digital signatures for contract formation.
summary: Map how offer and acceptance apply to clickwrap and browsewrap agreements. The Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553) validates electronic records and electronic/digital signatures for contract formation.
citations: Map how offer and acceptance apply to clickwrap and browsewrap agreements. The Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553)

section_title: Cyber Law and Emerging Technologies (Modern Modalities)
title: Smart Contracts & Blockchain
text: Smart Contracts & Blockchain: Define smart contracts as "agreements in digital form that are self-executing and self-enforcing". The graph should account for the legal characterization of digital tokens (e.g., as virtual commodities, securities, or choses in action/property).
summary: Define smart contracts as "agreements in digital form that are self-executing and self-enforcing". The graph should account for the legal characterization of digital tokens (e.g., as virtual commodities, securities, or choses in action/property).

section_title: Cyber Law and Emerging Technologies (Modern Modalities)
title: Data Privacy in Contracts
text: Data Privacy in Contracts: The Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (Cap. 486) interacts heavily with cloud computing and IT outsourcing contracts, requiring data users to bind third-party processors contractually to safeguard data.
summary: The Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (Cap. 486) interacts heavily with cloud computing and IT outsourcing contracts, requiring data users to bind third-party processors contractually to safeguard data.

title: Choice of Law
text: Choice of Law: If parties make an express agreement on governing law, it is generally respected. Without it, courts seek an implied choice or apply the system of law with the "closest and most real connection" to the transaction (evaluating place of performance, residence, etc.).
summary: If parties make an express agreement on governing law, it is generally respected. Without it, courts seek an implied choice or apply the system of law with the "closest and most real connection" to the transaction (evaluating place of performance, residence, etc.).

title: Long-Arm Jurisdiction
text: Long-Arm Jurisdiction: Under RHC O.11, Hong Kong courts can take jurisdiction over overseas defendants if the contract was made in HK, governed by HK law, or breached in HK, subject to forum non conveniens discretion.
summary: Under RHC O.11, Hong Kong courts can take jurisdiction over overseas defendants if the contract was made in HK, governed by HK law, or breached in HK, subject to forum non conveniens discretion.

title: Foreign Illegality
text: Foreign Illegality: A contract may be unenforceable in Hong Kong if its performance requires violating the laws of a friendly foreign state, based on international comity.
summary: A contract may be unenforceable in Hong Kong if its performance requires violating the laws of a friendly foreign state, based on international comity.

title: Arbitration
text: Arbitration: Map the Arbitration Ordinance (Cap. 609), which enforces valid arbitration agreements and excludes court jurisdiction. The tribunal has the power to rule on its own jurisdiction (kompetenz-kompetenz).
summary: Map the Arbitration Ordinance (Cap. 609), which enforces valid arbitration agreements and excludes court jurisdiction. The tribunal has the power to rule on its own jurisdiction (kompetenz-kompetenz).

title: Mediation
text: Mediation: Governed by the Mediation Ordinance (Cap. 620), which protects the strict confidentiality of mediation communications. Mention hybrid models like "Arb-Med".
summary: Governed by the Mediation Ordinance (Cap. 620), which protects the strict confidentiality of mediation communications. Mention hybrid models like "Arb-Med".

title: While not strictly contract law, this is the
text: While not strictly contract law, this is the vital fallback when a contract is void, frustrated, or rescinded. The cause of action requires showing that: (1) the defendant was enriched, (2) it was at the plaintiff's expense, and (3) there is an unjust factor (such as mistake, duress, or a total failure of consideration).
summary: While not strictly contract law, this is the vital fallback when a contract is void, frustrated, or rescinded. The cause of action requires showing that: (1) the defendant was enriched, (2) it was at the plaintiff's expense, and (3) there is an unjust factor (such as mistake, duress, or a total failure of consideration).

title: By structuring your GraphRAG to allow specific case
text: By structuring your GraphRAG to allow specific case nodes (e.g., Brinkibon for electronic acceptance) to link both to "Contract Formation" and "Conflict of Laws (Jurisdiction)", you will create a highly efficient, multi-dimensional legal research engine.
summary: By structuring your GraphRAG to allow specific case nodes (e.g., Brinkibon for electronic acceptance) to link both to "Contract Formation" and "Conflict of Laws (Jurisdiction)", you will create a highly efficient, multi-dimensional legal research engine.

title: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co
text: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co: Offers to the whole world are valid and can be accepted by performance of the requested act without prior communication,.
summary: Offers to the whole world are valid and can be accepted by performance of the requested act without prior communication,.
citations: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co

title: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd
text: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd: Performance of an existing contractual duty can constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor.
summary: Performance of an existing contractual duty can constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor.

title: Brinkibon Ltd v Stahag Stahl
text: Brinkibon Ltd v Stahag Stahl: For instantaneous electronic communications, acceptance is generally effective where and when it is received, determining the place of contract formation.
summary: For instantaneous electronic communications, acceptance is generally effective where and when it is received, determining the place of contract formation.

title: Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society
text: Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society: Objective interpretation of contracts; courts must ascertain the meaning conveyed to a reasonable person possessing all background knowledge reasonably available to the parties,.
summary: Objective interpretation of contracts; courts must ascertain the meaning conveyed to a reasonable person possessing all background knowledge reasonably available to the parties,.
citations: Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society

title: BP Refinery (Westernport) Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings
text: BP Refinery (Westernport) Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings: Sets the five-condition test for implying terms in fact, including that the term must be reasonable, necessary to give business efficacy, and so obvious it "goes without saying",.
summary: Sets the five-condition test for implying terms in fact, including that the term must be reasonable, necessary to give business efficacy, and so obvious it "goes without saying",.
citations: Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings

title: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd
text: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd: Established innominate (intermediate) terms; the right to terminate depends on whether the consequences of the breach deprive the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract,.
summary: Established innominate (intermediate) terms; the right to terminate depends on whether the consequences of the breach deprive the innocent party of substantially the whole benefit of the contract,.
citations: Hong Kong Fir Shipping Co Ltd v Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha Ltd

section_title: Vitiating Factors (Mistake, Misrepresentation, Duress, Undue Influence)
title: Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No 2)
text: Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge (No 2): The leading authority on undue influence; it clarifies that banks are "put on inquiry" in non-commercial surety relationships and must take reasonable steps to ensure the surety understands the risks through independent legal advice,,.
summary: The leading authority on undue influence; it clarifies that banks are "put on inquiry" in non-commercial surety relationships and must take reasonable steps to ensure the surety understands the risks through independent legal advice,,.
citations: Royal Bank of Scotland plc v Etridge

section_title: Vitiating Factors (Mistake, Misrepresentation, Duress, Undue Influence)
title: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long
text: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long: Established the modern framework for economic duress, requiring illegitimate pressure that amounts to a coercion of the will and leaves the victim with no practical alternative.
summary: Established the modern framework for economic duress, requiring illegitimate pressure that amounts to a coercion of the will and leaves the victim with no practical alternative.
citations: Pao On v Lau Yiu Long

section_title: Vitiating Factors (Mistake, Misrepresentation, Duress, Undue Influence)
title: Barton v Armstrong
text: Barton v Armstrong: In duress to the person, the threat need only be a cause of entering the contract, shifting the burden to the defendant to prove it did not contribute,.
summary: In duress to the person, the threat need only be a cause of entering the contract, shifting the burden to the defendant to prove it did not contribute,.

section_title: Vitiating Factors (Mistake, Misrepresentation, Duress, Undue Influence)
title: Lo Wo v Cheung Chan Ka
text: Lo Wo v Cheung Chan Ka: Relief for unconscionable bargains requires a serious disadvantage on the part of the exploitee, oppressive terms, and morally culpable conduct by the stronger party taking advantage of the opportunity,.
summary: Relief for unconscionable bargains requires a serious disadvantage on the part of the exploitee, oppressive terms, and morally culpable conduct by the stronger party taking advantage of the opportunity,.
citations: Lo Wo v Cheung Chan Ka

section_title: Vitiating Factors (Mistake, Misrepresentation, Duress, Undue Influence)
title: Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson / Cundy v Lindsay
text: Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson / Cundy v Lindsay: Unilateral mistake as to identity in written, arm's-length contracts renders the contract void, protecting title against innocent third-party purchasers.
summary: Unilateral mistake as to identity in written, arm's-length contracts renders the contract void, protecting title against innocent third-party purchasers.

title: Taylor v Caldwell
text: Taylor v Caldwell: Established the doctrine of frustration based on the destruction of the contract's essential subject matter.
summary: Established the doctrine of frustration based on the destruction of the contract's essential subject matter.

title: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC
text: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC: The modern test for frustration; it occurs only when a supervening event makes performance a radically different obligation from what was originally undertaken.
summary: The modern test for frustration; it occurs only when a supervening event makes performance a radically different obligation from what was originally undertaken.
citations: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC

title: Hadley v Baxendale
text: Hadley v Baxendale: The definitive test for remoteness of damage; losses are only recoverable if they arise naturally from the breach or were within the reasonable contemplation of both parties when contracting,.
summary: The definitive test for remoteness of damage; losses are only recoverable if they arise naturally from the breach or were within the reasonable contemplation of both parties when contracting,.

title: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage & Motor Co Ltd
text: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage & Motor Co Ltd: Liquidated damages are enforceable only if they are a genuine pre-estimate of loss, distinguishing them from unenforceable penalties,.
summary: Liquidated damages are enforceable only if they are a genuine pre-estimate of loss, distinguishing them from unenforceable penalties,.
citations: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v New Garage & Motor Co Ltd

title: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi
text: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi: Revised the penalty rule; a clause is an unenforceable penalty if it is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party in enforcing performance.
summary: Revised the penalty rule; a clause is an unenforceable penalty if it is out of all proportion to any legitimate interest of the innocent party in enforcing performance.
citations: Cavendish Square Holding BV v Talal El Makdessi

title: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth
text: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth: Courts may award damages for loss of amenity where the cost of cure for a breach is wholly disproportionate to the actual loss suffered,.
summary: Courts may award damages for loss of amenity where the cost of cure for a breach is wholly disproportionate to the actual loss suffered,.
citations: Ruxley Electronics and Construction Ltd v Forsyth

title: Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd
text: Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd: Allows for negotiating damages (restitutionary damages) based on the hypothetical sum the innocent party would have charged to release the defendant from a negative covenant.
summary: Allows for negotiating damages (restitutionary damages) based on the hypothetical sum the innocent party would have charged to release the defendant from a negative covenant.
citations: Wrotham Park Estate Co Ltd v Parkside Homes Ltd

title: Tweddle v Atkinson & Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co
text: Tweddle v Atkinson & Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd: Established the strict doctrine of privity, dictating that only parties to a contract can sue or be sued on it, and consideration must move from the promisee.
summary: Tweddle v Atkinson & Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd: Established the strict doctrine of privity, dictating that only parties to a contract can sue or be sued on it, and consideration must move from the promisee.
citations: Tweddle v Atkinson & Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd

title: Bonython v Commonwealth of Australia
text: Bonython v Commonwealth of Australia: In the absence of an express or implied choice of law, the governing law is the system with the "closest and most real connection" to the contract.
summary: In the absence of an express or implied choice of law, the governing law is the system with the "closest and most real connection" to the contract.

title: Lipkin Gorman v Karpnale Ltd
text: Lipkin Gorman v Karpnale Ltd: Formally recognized unjust enrichment as an independent cause of action and established the defense of "change of position",.
summary: Formally recognized unjust enrichment as an independent cause of action and established the defense of "change of position",.

title: Fibrosa Spolka Akcyjna v Fairbairn Lawson Combe Barbour Ltd
text: Fibrosa Spolka Akcyjna v Fairbairn Lawson Combe Barbour Ltd: Allows for restitution of money paid when there is a total failure of consideration following the frustration of a contract.
summary: Allows for restitution of money paid when there is a total failure of consideration following the frustration of a contract.
citations: Fibrosa Spolka Akcyjna v Fairbairn Lawson Combe Barbour Ltd

title: Fisher v Bell [1961]
text: Fisher v Bell [1961]: Display of goods with a price tag in a shop window is an invitation to treat, not an offer. (Cited in student tutorial material; requires HKLII retrieval for paragraph anchors)
summary: Display of goods with a price tag in a shop window is an invitation to treat, not an offer. (Cited in student tutorial material; requires HKLII retrieval for paragraph anchors)

title: Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists [1953]
text: Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists [1953]: Self-service display of goods is invitation to treat. (Standard authority; retrieval needed)
summary: Self-service display of goods is invitation to treat. (Standard authority; retrieval needed)
citations: Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists

title: Hong Kong CLIC Scenario 1
text: Hong Kong CLIC Scenario 1: Confirms that an advertisement is generally only an invitation to treat unless there is other evidence to the contrary
summary: Confirms that an advertisement is generally only an invitation to treat unless there is other evidence to the contrary

title: Communication of Acceptance & Postal Rule
text: Communication of Acceptance & Postal Rule
summary: Grouping node inside Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

title: Adams v Lindsell (1818)
text: Adams v Lindsell (1818): Established that acceptance by post takes effect upon posting (postal rule) when the letter is properly stamped and addressed. (Cited in tutorial; requires HKLII retrieval)
summary: Established that acceptance by post takes effect upon posting (postal rule) when the letter is properly stamped and addressed. (Cited in tutorial; requires HKLII retrieval)

title: Brinkibon Ltd v Stahag Stahl [1983]
text: Brinkibon Ltd v Stahag Stahl [1983]: For instantaneous electronic communications (telex/fax), acceptance is effective where and when it is received. (Requires retrieval)
summary: For instantaneous electronic communications (telex/fax), acceptance is effective where and when it is received. (Requires retrieval)

title: Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553) s.17
text: Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553) s.17: Provides that contracts may be formed electronically unless parties agree otherwise, with certain excluded documents listed in Schedule 1
summary: Provides that contracts may be formed electronically unless parties agree otherwise, with certain excluded documents listed in Schedule 1

title: Consideration
text: Consideration
summary: Grouping node inside Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

title: Currie v Misa (1875)
text: Currie v Misa (1875): Defined consideration as "some benefit, interest or profit accruing to one party, or some forbearance, detriment, loss or responsibility given, suffered or undertaken by the other"
summary: Defined consideration as "some benefit, interest or profit accruing to one party, or some forbearance, detriment, loss or responsibility given, suffered or undertaken by the other"

title: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd [1915]
text: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd [1915]: Defined consideration as "the price for which the promise of the other is bought"
summary: Defined consideration as "the price for which the promise of the other is bought"
citations: Dunlop Pneumatic Tyre Co Ltd v Selfridge & Co Ltd

title: Thomas v Thomas (1842)
text: Thomas v Thomas (1842): Consideration must be sufficient (have some value in law) but need not be adequate
summary: Consideration must be sufficient (have some value in law) but need not be adequate

title: Chappel & Co Ltd v Nestle Co Ltd [1960]
text: Chappel & Co Ltd v Nestle Co Ltd [1960]: A peppercorn can be good consideration even if the promisee does not like pepper
summary: A peppercorn can be good consideration even if the promisee does not like pepper
citations: Chappel & Co Ltd v Nestle Co Ltd

title: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd [1991]
text: Williams v Roffey Bros & Nicholls (Contractors) Ltd [1991]: Performance of an existing contractual duty can constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor
summary: Performance of an existing contractual duty can constitute valid consideration if it confers a "practical benefit" on the promisor

title: Roscorla v Thomas (1842)
text: Roscorla v Thomas (1842): Past consideration is not valid consideration
summary: Past consideration is not valid consideration

title: Re McArdle [1951]
text: Re McArdle [1951]: Past consideration - promise of payment after performance is not binding
summary: Past consideration - promise of payment after performance is not binding

title: Stilk v Myrick (1809)
text: Stilk v Myrick (1809): Existing contractual duty does not constitute valid consideration
summary: Existing contractual duty does not constitute valid consideration

title: Hartley v Ponsonby [1857]
text: Hartley v Ponsonby [1857]: Going beyond existing contractual duty constitutes valid consideration
summary: Going beyond existing contractual duty constitutes valid consideration

title: Pinnel's Case (1602)
text: Pinnel's Case (1602): Part payment of a debt is not valid consideration for a promise to discharge the whole debt, unless paid before the due date, with a chattel, or to a different destination
summary: Part payment of a debt is not valid consideration for a promise to discharge the whole debt, unless paid before the due date, with a chattel, or to a different destination

title: Foakes v Beer (1884)
text: Foakes v Beer (1884): Affirmed that part payment of a debt does not discharge the whole obligation
summary: Affirmed that part payment of a debt does not discharge the whole obligation

title: Intention to Create Legal Relations
text: Intention to Create Legal Relations
summary: Grouping node inside Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

title: Stephen Chiau Sing Chi v Alice Yu Man
text: Stephen Chiau Sing Chi v Alice Yu Man Fung (Hong Kong case, 2020-2021): Court held that a profit-sharing agreement made between romantic partners in a social setting was not intended to be legally binding. The romantic relationship context, vague language, and lack of commercial sense were key factors
summary: Stephen Chiau Sing Chi v Alice Yu Man Fung (Hong Kong case, 2020-2021): Court held that a profit-sharing agreement made between romantic partners in a social setting was not intended to be legally binding. The romantic relationship context, vague language, and lack of commercial sense were key factors
citations: Stephen Chiau Sing Chi v Alice Yu Man Fung

title: Presumption in commercial contexts
text: Presumption in commercial contexts: The general rule is that parties intend to create legal relations in commercial agreements
summary: The general rule is that parties intend to create legal relations in commercial agreements

title: Presumption in domestic/social settings
text: Presumption in domestic/social settings: The general rule is that there is no intention to create legal relations in familial or romantic relationships, but this can be rebutted by surrounding circumstances
summary: The general rule is that there is no intention to create legal relations in familial or romantic relationships, but this can be rebutted by surrounding circumstances

title: Capacity
text: Capacity
summary: Grouping node inside Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

title: Peters v Fleming (1840)
text: Peters v Fleming (1840): A minor can be required to pay a reasonable price for necessaries, defined as items necessary to maintain the minor in the "station of life in which he moves"
summary: A minor can be required to pay a reasonable price for necessaries, defined as items necessary to maintain the minor in the "station of life in which he moves"

title: General rule
text: General rule: Minors are protected by common law and statutory rules; contracts with minors are generally unenforceable against the minor except for (1) necessaries, (2) beneficial contracts of employment/service, and (3) voidable contracts (shares, partnerships, leases)
summary: Minors are protected by common law and statutory rules; contracts with minors are generally unenforceable against the minor except for (1) necessaries, (2) beneficial contracts of employment/service, and (3) voidable contracts (shares, partnerships, leases)

title: Formality
text: Formality
summary: Grouping node inside Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

text: Conveyancing and Property Ordinance (Cap. 219) s.3(1): Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be in writing or evidenced by a signed memorandum. (Statutory authority)
summary: Contracts for the sale or disposition of land must be in writing or evidenced by a signed memorandum. (Statutory authority)

title: Tang Kwai Cheung v Yeung Sze Ting [2024] HKCA 383
text: Tang Kwai Cheung v Yeung Sze Ting [2024] HKCA 383: Recent Court of Appeal case addressing rescission rights and implied terms in a sale and purchase agreement for property. The SPA contained a conditional clause regarding grant of letters of administration
summary: Recent Court of Appeal case addressing rescission rights and implied terms in a sale and purchase agreement for property. The SPA contained a conditional clause regarding grant of letters of administration

title: Cheng Kwei Sheng v Li Shek Tong [1976] HKCA
text: Cheng Kwei Sheng v Li Shek Tong [1976] HKCA: Court of Appeal addressed the extent to which extrinsic evidence is admissible to interpret a written document. Held that where language is clear and unambiguous, evidence of surrounding circumstances is not admissible to contradict the written terms. However, where evidence shows an apparent ambiguity, surrounding circumstances may be considered
summary: Court of Appeal addressed the extent to which extrinsic evidence is admissible to interpret a written document. Held that where language is clear and unambiguous, evidence of surrounding circumstances is not admissible to contradict the written terms. However, where evidence shows an apparent ambiguity, surrounding circumstances may be considered
citations: Cheng Kwei Sheng v Li Shek Tong

title: Prenn v Simmonds [1971] (HL)
text: Prenn v Simmonds [1971] (HL): Evidence of negotiations and parties' intentions is not admissible; evidence should be restricted to the factual background known to the parties at or before the contract date
summary: Evidence of negotiations and parties' intentions is not admissible; evidence should be restricted to the factual background known to the parties at or before the contract date

title: River Wear Commissioners v Adamson (1877)
text: River Wear Commissioners v Adamson (1877): The meaning of words varies according to circumstances; courts must inquire beyond language to see what circumstances the words were used in reference to
summary: The meaning of words varies according to circumstances; courts must inquire beyond language to see what circumstances the words were used in reference to

title: Implied Terms
text: Implied Terms
summary: Grouping node inside Restitution and Unjust Enrichment.

title: BP Refinery (Westernport) Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings (1977)
text: BP Refinery (Westernport) Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings (1977): Established the five-condition test for implying terms in fact: (1) reasonable and equitable, (2) necessary to give business efficacy, (3) so obvious it "goes without saying" (officious bystander), (4) capable of clear expression, (5) not contradictory to express terms
summary: Established the five-condition test for implying terms in fact: (1) reasonable and equitable, (2) necessary to give business efficacy, (3) so obvious it "goes without saying" (officious bystander), (4) capable of clear expression, (5) not contradictory to express terms
citations: Pty Ltd v Shire of Hastings

title: Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities
text: Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services Trust Co (Jersey) Ltd [2016] (UKSC): Clarified that business necessity and obviousness are alternative requirements (only one need be satisfied). A term should not be implied merely because it appears fair or reasonable
summary: Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services Trust Co (Jersey) Ltd [2016] (UKSC): Clarified that business necessity and obviousness are alternative requirements (only one need be satisfied). A term should not be implied merely because it appears fair or reasonable
citations: Marks and Spencer plc v BNP Paribas Securities Services Trust Co

title: Ask Re Ltd v Grain Mineral Resources Pte Ltd [2015] HKCFI 1232
text: Ask Re Ltd v Grain Mineral Resources Pte Ltd [2015] HKCFI 1232: Hong Kong Court of First Instance implied a term that parties to a documentary credit transaction would rely only on documents known or believed to be genuine. The seller submitted false documents, constituting repudiatory breach
summary: Hong Kong Court of First Instance implied a term that parties to a documentary credit transaction would rely only on documents known or believed to be genuine. The seller submitted false documents, constituting repudiatory breach
citations: Ask Re Ltd v Grain Mineral Resources Pte Ltd

title: Yifung Developments Ltd v Liu Chi Keung Ricky [2016] HKCFI 678
text: Yifung Developments Ltd v Liu Chi Keung Ricky [2016] HKCFI 678: Hong Kong case applying Marks and Spencer, reinforcing that business efficacy and officious bystander tests are alternatives, and mere reasonableness does not justify implying a term
summary: Hong Kong case applying Marks and Spencer, reinforcing that business efficacy and officious bystander tests are alternatives, and mere reasonableness does not justify implying a term
citations: Yifung Developments Ltd v Liu Chi Keung Ricky

title: Officious bystander test
text: Officious bystander test: A term may be implied if it is so obvious that "it goes without saying" that both parties would have agreed to it had an officious bystander suggested it
summary: A term may be implied if it is so obvious that "it goes without saying" that both parties would have agreed to it had an officious bystander suggested it
